1	//! Auxiliary functions to manipulate prototypes and closures.
2	//!
3	//! Port of `reference/lua-5.4.7/src/lfunc.c` (295 lines, 16 functions).
4	//! The companion header `lfunc.h` is merged here per PORTING.md §1.
5	//!
6	//! # Design notes
7	//!
8	//! The C implementation uses two intrusive linked lists managed through pointer
9	//! fields embedded in stack slots and upvalue objects:
10	//!
11	//! - **`openupval`**: a singly-linked list of `UpVal`s sorted by stack level
12	//!   (highest first), threaded through `UpVal.u.open.next / .previous`.
13	//! - **`tbclist`**: a to-be-closed variable list encoded as `unsigned short` delta
14	//!   offsets stored inside `StackValue.tbclist.delta`.
15	//!
16	//! Both are replaced in the Rust port:
17	//! - `openupval` → `LuaState.openupval: Vec<GcRef<UpVal>>` (descending by StackIdx).
18	//! - `tbclist`   → `LuaState.tbclist: Vec<StackIdx>` (back = most recent entry).
19	//!
20	//! The delta-encoding machinery (MAXDELTA, dummy nodes) is an artifact of the u16
21	//! delta field and is entirely superseded by the `Vec<StackIdx>` model.
22	
23	// PORT NOTE: `LuaProto` is currently a stub in crate::state (from lstate.c's
24	// partial port in state.rs). The full `LuaProto` definition belongs in
25	// crate::object (lobject.c → object.rs). Fields referenced below will compile
26	// once object.rs is written; see TODO(port) at each field site.
27	
28	// PORT NOTE: `GcRef<T> = Rc<T>` in Phase A–C provides no interior mutability.
29	// `close_upval` and `init_upvals` must mutate `UpVal` and `LuaClosure` values
30	// that are shared through `GcRef`. In Phase B, the design options are:
31	//   (a) `GcRef<T> = Rc<RefCell<T>>` for mutable GC objects, or
32	//   (b) a custom `GcCell<T>` wrapper with conditional interior mutability.
33	// Both `close_upval` and `init_upvals` carry `TODO(port)` at the mutation sites.
34	
35	use std::rc::Rc;
36	#[allow(unused_imports)] use crate::prelude::*;
37	
38	use crate::{
39	    state::{
40	        GcRef, LuaClosureC, LuaClosureLua, LuaState, LuaValue, UpVal,
41	    },
42	    tagmethods::TagMethod,
43	};
44	// TODO(port): import paths will stabilize in Phase B. LuaError lives in
45	// lua_types::error once that crate is populated; for now we import from crate::state.
46	use lua_types::error::LuaError;
47	pub use lua_types::{CallInfoIdx, StackIdx};
48	
49	// ── lfunc.h constants ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
50	
51	// C: #define CLOSEKTOP (-1)  (lfunc.h)
52	// macros.tsv: CLOSEKTOP → const CLOSE_K_TOP: i32 = -1
53	/// Sentinel status meaning "close upvalues but preserve the stack top."
54	/// Passed as `status` to `close` / `prep_call_close_mth`.
55	pub(crate) const CLOSE_K_TOP: i32 = -1;
56	
57	// C: #define MAXUPVAL 255  (lfunc.h)
58	// macros.tsv: MAXUPVAL → const MAX_UPVAL: u8 = 255
59	/// Maximum number of upvalues in a single closure (Lua or C).
60	/// The value must fit in a VM register (u8).
61	pub(crate) const MAX_UPVAL: u8 = 255;
62	
63	// C: #define MAXMISS 10  (lfunc.h)
64	// macros.tsv: MAXMISS → const MAX_MISS: u32 = 10
65	/// Maximum consecutive misses before giving up the closure cache in `LuaProto`.
66	pub(crate) const MAX_MISS: u32 = 10;
67	
68	// ── Closure allocation ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
69	
70	/// Allocates a new C closure with `nupvals` upvalue slots, all initialised to
71	/// `LuaValue::Nil`.
72	///
73	/// The caller is responsible for setting the function pointer (`f`) and
74	/// populating the upvalue slots before exposing the closure to Lua code.
75	///
76	/// C: `CClosure *luaF_newCclosure(lua_State *L, int nupvals)`
77	pub(crate) fn new_c_closure(
78	    state: &mut LuaState,
79	    nupvals: u8,
80	) -> GcRef<crate::state::LuaClosure> {
81	    // C: GCObject *o = luaC_newobj(L, LUA_VCCL, sizeCclosure(nupvals));
82	    //    CClosure *c = gco2ccl(o);
83	    //    c->nupvalues = cast_byte(nupvals);
84	    //    return c;
85	    //
86	    // sizeCclosure is a C allocation-size helper; dropped — Vec handles sizing.
87	    // cast_byte(nupvals) → nupvals as u8 (already u8).
88	    // luaC_newobj → state.gc().new_obj(...) — in Phase A–C just Rc allocation.
89	    // gco2ccl → gc.cast_c_closure() — unnecessary in Rust, enum variant is the cast.
90	    //
91	    // TODO(port): LuaClosureC.f must be set by the caller. The C pattern allocates
92	    // then immediately assigns `c->f = fn`. Either make `f: Option<LuaCFunction>` in
93	    // LuaClosureC, or add a `new_c_closure(state, nupvals, f)` parameter. For now we
94	    // store a dummy; reconcile in Phase B.
95	    let closure = crate::state::LuaClosure::C(GcRef::new(LuaClosureC {
96	        // C: c->f is set by caller; placeholder index 0 here. LuaCFnPtr is a
97	        // registry index (see lua-types::closure); the caller overwrites it.
98	        func: DUMMY_C_FUNCTION_IDX,
99	        upvalues: vec![LuaValue::Nil; nupvals as usize],
100	    }));
101	    // C: luaC_newobj registers with the GC. In Phase A–C this is Rc::new.
102	    GcRef::new(closure)
103	}
104	
105	/// Allocates a new Lua closure with `nupvals` upvalue slots (all `None`).
106	///
107	/// The caller must set the `proto` field and populate `upvals` before the
108	/// closure is executed.
109	///
110	/// C: `LClosure *luaF_newLclosure(lua_State *L, int nupvals)`
111	pub(crate) fn new_lua_closure(
112	    state: &mut LuaState,
113	    nupvals: u8,
114	) -> GcRef<crate::state::LuaClosure> {
115	    // C: GCObject *o = luaC_newobj(L, LUA_VLCL, sizeLclosure(nupvals));
116	    //    LClosure *c = gco2lcl(o);
117	    //    c->p = NULL;
118	    //    c->nupvalues = cast_byte(nupvals);
119	    //    while (nupvals--) c->upvals[nupvals] = NULL;
120	    //    return c;
121	    //
122	    // sizeLclosure → dropped (Vec handles sizing).
123	    // c->p = NULL → proto field will be set by caller.
124	    // TODO(port): LuaClosureLua.proto is GcRef<LuaProto> (non-optional per types.tsv).
125	    // The C code allows NULL here (set later). Either use Option<GcRef<LuaProto>> in
126	    // the Rust struct, or require proto at construction time. Reconcile in Phase B.
127	    // For Phase A we use a sentinel value; this line will not compile as-is.
128	    let _ = state; // state used for GC registration in Phase D
129	    let _ = nupvals;
130	    // TODO(phase-b): LuaClosureLua.proto is non-optional; need a placeholder
131	    // until the caller assigns. Using LuaProto::placeholder() for Phase B compile.
132	    let lcl = GcRef::new(LuaClosureLua::placeholder());
133	    let closure = crate::state::LuaClosure::Lua(lcl);
134	    GcRef::new(closure)
135	}
136	
137	/// Fills a Lua closure's upvalue slots with freshly-allocated closed upvalues,
138	/// each holding `LuaValue::Nil`. Used when compiling closures that capture no
139	/// live stack variables.
140	///
141	/// C: `void luaF_initupvals(lua_State *L, LClosure *cl)`
142	pub(crate) fn init_upvals(state: &mut LuaState, cl: &GcRef<lua_types::LuaLClosure>) -> Result<(), LuaError> {
143	    // C: for (i = 0; i < cl->nupvalues; i++) {
144	    //      GCObject *o = luaC_newobj(L, LUA_VUPVAL, sizeof(UpVal));
145	    //      UpVal *uv = gco2upv(o);
146	    //      uv->v.p = &uv->u.value;  /* make it closed */
147	    //      setnilvalue(uv->v.p);    /* *o = LuaValue::Nil */
148	    //      cl->upvals[i] = uv;
149	    //      luaC_objbarrier(L, cl, uv);
150	    //  }
151	    //
152	    // In Rust: create UpVal::Closed(Nil) for each slot; GC barrier is no-op Phase A–C.
153	
154	    // TODO(port): GcRef<T> = Rc<T> has no interior mutability. Mutating
155	    // `cl.upvals[i]` here requires either Rc<RefCell<LuaClosure>> or Rc::get_mut.
156	    // The code below captures the intended logic; it will not compile until
157	    // GcRef provides a borrow_mut() path (Phase B design decision).
158	    let n = cl.upvals.len();
159	    for i in 0..n {
160	        // C: luaC_newobj(L, LUA_VUPVAL, sizeof(UpVal)) → Rc::new(UpVal::Closed(Nil))
161	        let uv: GcRef<UpVal> = GcRef::new(UpVal::closed(LuaValue::Nil));
162	        // TODO(port): cl.borrow_mut().as_lua_mut().upvals[i] = Some(uv.clone());
163	        // Requires interior mutability; see PORT NOTE at top of file.
164	        let _ = (i, uv);
165	        // C: luaC_objbarrier(L, cl, uv) → state.gc().obj_barrier(cl, &uv) — no-op Phase A–C
166	    }
167	    let _ = state; // used for GC barrier in Phase D
168	    Ok(())
169	}
170	
171	// ── Open-upvalue management ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
172	
173	/// Creates a new open upvalue for stack slot `level`, inserts it into
174	/// `state.openupval` at `insert_pos`, and registers the thread in the
175	/// global `twups` list if necessary.
176	///
177	/// C: `static UpVal *newupval(lua_State *L, StkId level, UpVal **prev)`
178	fn new_open_upval(
179	    state: &mut LuaState,
180	    level: StackIdx,
181	    insert_pos: usize,
182	) -> GcRef<UpVal> {
183	    // C: GCObject *o = luaC_newobj(L, LUA_VUPVAL, sizeof(UpVal));
184	    //    UpVal *uv = gco2upv(o);
185	    //    UpVal *next = *prev;
186	    //    uv->v.p = s2v(level);   /* current value lives in the stack */
187	    //    uv->u.open.next = next;
188	    //    uv->u.open.previous = prev;
189	    //    if (next) next->u.open.previous = &uv->u.open.next;
190	    //    *prev = uv;
191	    //
192	    // In Rust: intrusive next/previous fields are gone; Vec insertion replaces
193	    // the pointer-threading. The `prev` parameter (UpVal **) becomes `insert_pos`.
194	    //
195	    // C: UpVal.v.p = s2v(level) → UpVal::Open { thread_id: 0, idx: level }
196	    // The `thread` component of PORT_STRATEGY §3.8 is deferred to Phase E (coroutines).
197	    // macros.tsv: uplevel → thread_stack_idx field of Open variant.
198	    let uv: GcRef<UpVal> = GcRef::new(UpVal::open(0, level));
199	    // PORT NOTE: Vec insert maintains descending StackIdx order (highest first),
200	    // mirroring the C intrusive list where the head is always the topmost slot.

680	const DUMMY_C_FUNCTION_IDX: crate::state::LuaCFnPtr = usize::MAX;
681	
682	/// Returns `true` if this thread is already registered in `global.twups`.
683	///
684	/// C: `isintwups(L)` → `L->twups != L` (intrusive list: thread is in twups
685	/// iff its twups pointer doesn't point back to itself).
686	///
687	/// TODO(port): In Rust, global.twups is `Vec<GcRef<LuaState>>`. Membership
688	/// check requires either a flag on LuaState or a scan. This helper is a stub
689	/// returning `false` (safe conservative answer: always re-insert) until the
690	/// coroutine / twups design is finalised in Phase E.
691	fn state_in_twups(state: &LuaState) -> bool {
692	    // TODO(port): implement membership test in global_state.twups Vec.
693	    // Requires a GcRef<LuaState> to self — self-referential Rc, unsolved until Phase E.
694	    // Returning false means new_open_upval will always attempt re-insertion,
695	    // which is safe (duplicate handling is deferred).
696	    let _ = state;
697	    false
698	}
699	
700	// ── Trait stubs needed for compilation ───────────────────────────────────────
701	
702	/// Stub methods on `LuaState` assumed by this module.
703	///
704	/// These will be implemented in their home modules (do_.rs, debug.rs, tagmethods.rs)
705	/// and removed from this file in Phase B.
706	impl LuaState {
707	    /// Returns the `LuaValue` at stack index `idx`.
708	    ///
709	    /// C: `s2v(level)` (access the TValue inside a StackValue).
710	    /// macros.tsv: `s2v → state.stack_at(idx)`.
711	    pub(crate) fn get_stack_value(&self, idx: StackIdx) -> &LuaValue {
712	        // TODO(port): bounds-check and return &self.stack[idx.0 as usize].val
713	        &self.stack[idx.0 as usize].val
714	    }
715	
716	    /// Returns the current CallInfo (active call frame).
717	    ///
718	    /// C: `L->ci` (dereferenced).
719	    pub(crate) fn current_ci(&self) -> &crate::state::CallInfo {
720	        // TODO(port): return &self.call_info[self.ci.0 as usize]
721	        &self.call_info[self.ci.0 as usize]
722	    }
723	
724	    /// Looks up the `__close` (or other) metamethod for a value.
725	    ///
726	    /// C: `luaT_gettmbyobj(L, obj, TM_CLOSE)`.
727	    /// macros.tsv: `fasttm → state.fast_tm(et, e)`.
728	    /// TODO(port): real implementation in tagmethods.rs.
729	    pub(crate) fn get_tm_by_obj<T>(&self, _val: &LuaValue, _tm: T) -> LuaValue {

679:    // C: struct lua_State *twups — threads with open upvalues
680:    // types.tsv: global_State.twups → Vec<GcRef<LuaState>>
681:    pub twups: Vec<GcRef<LuaState>>,
875:    // C: UpVal *openupval — open upvalue list (was intrusive; now a Vec)
876:    // types.tsv: lua_State.openupval → Vec<GcRef<UpVal>>
877:    pub openupval: Vec<GcRef<UpVal>>,
2083:    // C: L->twups = L; /* thread has no upvalues */
2084:    // PORT NOTE: In C, L->twups = L is a self-reference sentinel meaning "no open upvals".
2085:    // In Rust, GlobalState.twups is a Vec<GcRef<LuaState>>; absence from that Vec is the
2086:    // sentinel.  The per-thread `twups` field is removed (types.tsv: lua_State.twups → removed).
2101:    // C: L->openupval = NULL;
2102:    thread.openupval = Vec::new();
2192:        openupval: Vec::new(),
2252:/// //   lua_assert(L1->openupval == NULL);
2263:    // C: lua_assert(L1->openupval == NULL);
2266:        thread.openupval.is_empty(),
2505:        // C: g->twups = NULL;
2506:        twups: Vec::new(),
2539:        openupval: Vec::new(),
2703://                  (3) lua_State.twups self-reference → membership in GlobalState.twups Vec.

delta.text:
Now I understand the bug. Let me trace through:

1. `find_upval
` at func.rs:222 has a debug assert: `state_in_twups(state) || state.openupval.is_empty()` (line

224).
2. `state_in_twups` at func.rs:691 **always returns `false`** (stub, see line 696).
3. So
as soon as **the second** open upvalue is needed (or any time `find_upval` is called when `openupval` is non-

empty), the assert fires.

The C invariant "thread in twups iff it has open upvalues" is a structural artifact of the C
intrusive list design. In our Phase A–D Vec-based design, with coroutines stubbed, there is effectively a
single thread that is conceptually always in twups. The actual `GlobalState.twups` Vec management is deferred to Phase D
/E (the comment at func.rs:208 makes this explicit). The simplest correct fix is to fl
ip `state_in_twups` to return `true`.

content.text:
Now I understand the bug. Let me trace through:

1. `find_upval` at func.rs:222 has a debug assert: `state_in_twups(state) || state.openupval.is_empty()` (line 224).
2. `state_in_twups` at func.rs:691 **always returns `false`** (stub, see line 696).
3. So as soon as **the second** open upvalue is needed (or any time `find_upval` is called when `openupval` is non-empty), the assert fires.

The C invariant "thread in twups iff it has open upvalues" is a structural artifact of the C intrusive list design. In our Phase A–D Vec-based design, with coroutines stubbed, there is effectively a single thread that is conceptually always in twups. The actual `GlobalState.twups` Vec management is deferred to Phase D/E (the comment at func.rs:208 makes this explicit). The simplest correct fix is to flip `state_in_twups` to return `true`.

lines: /// C: `isintwups(L)` → `L->twups != L` (intrusive list: thread is in twups
/// iff its twups pointer doesn't point back to itself).
-/// TODO(port): In Rust, global.twups is `Vec<GcRef<LuaState>>`. Membership
-/// check requires either a flag on LuaState or a scan. This helper is a stub
-/// returning `false` (safe conservative answer: always re-insert) until the
-/// coroutine / twups design is finalised in Phase E.
+/// PORT NOTE: In Phase A–D with coroutines stubbed there is effectively a
+/// single thread. The actual `GlobalState.twups` Vec management (insertion in
+/// `new_open_upval`) is deferred to Phase D/E and would require a GcRef-to-self.
+/// Until then we treat every thread as conceptually present in twups, which
+/// satisfies the invariant `state_in_twups || openupval.is_empty()` asserted by
+/// `find_upval`. The actual twups list does not yet drive any behaviour.
fn state_in_twups(state: &LuaState) -> bool {
-    // TODO(port): implement membership test in global_state.twups Vec.
-    // Requires a GcRef<LuaState> to self — self-referential Rc, unsolved until Phase E.
-    // Returning false means new_open_upval will always attempt re-insertion,
-    // which is safe (duplicate handling is deferred).
let _ = state;
-    false
+    true

input.command:
cargo run -q -p lua-cli -- 'local function partition(t, lo, hi)
    local pivot = t[hi]
    local i = lo - 1
    for j = lo, hi - 1 do
        if t[j] <= pivot then
            i = i + 1
            local tmp = t[i]; t[i] = t[j]; t[j] = tmp
        end
    end
    local tmp = t[i+1]; t[i+1] = t[hi]; t[hi] = tmp
    return i + 1
end

local function quicksort(t, lo, hi)
    if lo < hi then
        local p = partition(t, lo, hi)
        quicksort(t, lo, p - 1)
        quicksort(t, p + 1, hi)
    end
end

local arr = {5, 2, 8, 1, 9, 3}
quicksort(arr, 1, 6)
for i = 1, 6 do print(arr[i]) end' 2>&1 | tail -30

delta.text: The
test now succeeds cleanly — quicksort runs to completion and prints the sorted array `1, 2, 3

, 5, 8, 9`.

## Report

**Root cause:** `crates/lua-vm/src/func.rs:691` — `state_in_twups()` was a h
ard-coded stub returning `false`. The debug-assert at `find_upval` (line 224) checks `state_in_twups(state) || state
.openupval.is_empty()`, so any second call to `find_upval` (or any call when `openupval` was non-empty) t
ripped the assert. In the quicksort program, the recursive Lua closures `partition` and `quicksort` capture upvalues, tri

ggering `find_upval` on the populated list during the recursive descent.

**Fix:** Flip
ped `state_in_twups` to return `true`. The C `isintwups` invariant is a structural artifact of the C int
rusive linked-list design (where `L->twups == L` is a sentinel meaning "not in list
"). Our Vec-based design with stubbed coroutines (Phase A–D) has effectively one thread, and the act
ual `GlobalState.twups` Vec management is already deferred via `TODO(port)` at line 207-211
. Treating the thread as conceptually always-in-twups satisfies the invariant without affecting any real behaviour, because nothing currently cons

umes the twups list. Updated the doc comment to a `PORT NOTE` explaining this.

**New status:** No
new error — the test now passes and prints the sorted output `1 2 3 5 8 9` followed by `[

ok] execution completed, status=Ok`.

No temporary `eprintln!` was added (the call path was clear from the pan
ic message), so nothing to remove.

content.text:
The test now succeeds cleanly — quicksort runs to completion and prints the sorted array `1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 9`.

## Report

**Root cause:** `crates/lua-vm/src/func.rs:691` — `state_in_twups()` was a hard-coded stub returning `false`. The debug-assert at `find_upval` (line 224) checks `state_in_twups(state) || state.openupval.is_empty()`, so any second call to `find_upval` (or any call when `openupval` was non-empty) tripped the assert. In the quicksort program, the recursive Lua closures `partition` and `quicksort` capture upvalues, triggering `find_upval` on the populated list during the recursive descent.

**Fix:** Flipped `state_in_twups` to return `true`. The C `isintwups` invariant is a structural artifact of the C intrusive linked-list design (where `L->twups == L` is a sentinel meaning "not in list"). Our Vec-based design with stubbed coroutines (Phase A–D) has effectively one thread, and the actual `GlobalState.twups` Vec management is already deferred via `TODO(port)` at line 207-211. Treating the thread as conceptually always-in-twups satisfies the invariant without affecting any real behaviour, because nothing currently consumes the twups list. Updated the doc comment to a `PORT NOTE` explaining this.

**New status:** No new error — the test now passes and prints the sorted output `1 2 3 5 8 9` followed by `[ok] execution completed, status=Ok`.

No temporary `eprintln!` was added (the call path was clear from the panic message), so nothing to remove.

result:
The test now succeeds cleanly — quicksort runs to completion and prints the sorted array `1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 9`.

## Report

**Root cause:** `crates/lua-vm/src/func.rs:691` — `state_in_twups()` was a hard-coded stub returning `false`. The debug-assert at `find_upval` (line 224) checks `state_in_twups(state) || state.openupval.is_empty()`, so any second call to `find_upval` (or any call when `openupval` was non-empty) tripped the assert. In the quicksort program, the recursive Lua closures `partition` and `quicksort` capture upvalues, triggering `find_upval` on the populated list during the recursive descent.

**Fix:** Flipped `state_in_twups` to return `true`. The C `isintwups` invariant is a structural artifact of the C intrusive linked-list design (where `L->twups == L` is a sentinel meaning "not in list"). Our Vec-based design with stubbed coroutines (Phase A–D) has effectively one thread, and the actual `GlobalState.twups` Vec management is already deferred via `TODO(port)` at line 207-211. Treating the thread as conceptually always-in-twups satisfies the invariant without affecting any real behaviour, because nothing currently consumes the twups list. Updated the doc comment to a `PORT NOTE` explaining this.

**New status:** No new error — the test now passes and prints the sorted output `1 2 3 5 8 9` followed by `[ok] execution completed, status=Ok`.

No temporary `eprintln!` was added (the call path was clear from the panic message), so nothing to remove.
